speaker: interviewer
text: The interview with Lola Snow. It is the 10th of November, 1995. So Lola, you were taken to Sosnowitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: With young men and young women.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And there we underwent medical examination. And my mother must have had connection with the doctor who examined me because he declared me unfit for work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So somehow your mother had organized something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So three of us were unfit of work, one pregnant woman, one was the niece of the president of the Jewish Welfare, and myself. During the night, the same night, the sturmbannfuhrer came. His name, name was Lindbergh, Lindner, Lindner. He was limping. And he was a very frightening figure. We all were scared of him because we heard that he, he does atrocious things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see him do atrocious things?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He just divided the groups of the, of the girls. This groups goes to this lager, the camp. This group goes to this camp, and this group goes to this camp. And then he says, and those three don't belong to us. They belong to the secret police.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I heard this-- I was a young, youngster still-- I say, that's impossible that the German are going to forgive me. I committed a crime, so they won't let me free. They must probably put me in jail. When you, when you talk about crime, you mean the coming with no papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No, crossing the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Across the border.

speaker: interviewee
text: Crossing the border, that was the crime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, I said to myself, I'd rather go to the camp. I had no, no, connection with my mother. I had no, any, any way of communicating with her. So on, on my own, I decided it. And I went to the authorities there, and I said, I think I'm still well enough to go and work for a little while. And the same night, we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they put you with the group?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the group. And I also wanted to go with this group because at this time-- that was February 1942, 18th of February, 1942. At this time, you know, whoever could help themself in our city, had a bit of money or not, somehow they didn't go to the camp. And I was afraid that I'll come to a camp when I don't-- can't relate to the girls. In this group, there were girls that I knew, that they had some education, and I can talk to them. So I thought, I'll be much better if go with this group because they won't let me free on my own. I thought, that's impossible that they let me free. They'll do something with me which will be much worse than going with this group to the camp. And so I went to the camp. How we went and--

speaker: interviewer
text: I want you now to tell me that. How-- first of all, you, you were with this group of girls. You disobeyed your mother. You'd, you'd rejected her getting you out of this group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you thought it through and thought you'd be better with the group.

speaker: interviewee
text: With the group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, where was the group taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Ober Altstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get to Ober Altstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were put on trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- tell me about the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: The trains were not as bad as the trains to Auschwitz. They took a long time. There was not much food on it, but nobody died on that train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- how were you accommodated on the train? Were you in the-- were you-- did you have seats? Were you sitting on the floor? Were you standing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where there were room, on the floor, on the seats, you know? They, they were not really the trains which I see now that went to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: They weren't the cattle wagons.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were not the cattle wagons. I was very lucky because I stayed in the same camp for three years and three-- over three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: So let's, let's go back to the actual trip, though. So you were put on this train. There were only girls now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: What ages were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were most probably from 17 till 24, I would think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you travel for?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have food?

speaker: interviewee
text: I suppose I had. I don't remember. I don't remember. It wasn't a frightful [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] train trip as the one that went to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you arrived. Did you have any idea where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who guarded you?

speaker: interviewee
text: SS men.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had dogs? Do you remember dogs?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just SS men.

speaker: interviewee
text: SS men.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you remained on, on, on this, on this train until you arrived somewhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived somewhere, but I don't remember. I see myself in the camp as I arrive. But the trip from the station, wherever we, we got off to the camp, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to the camp. The camp was already established. There were already prior transport to this camp. The Lagerfuhrer was a, a very frightful person. Her name was Erma Hoffman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was she from?

speaker: interviewee
text: She must-- she either was from Sudeten, or she was a German. I don't know. But she was German. And she had a, she had a staff with her, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she ran the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: She ran the camp together with Jewish people who were in [NON-ENGLISH], a, a, a cook. Who else was there? A tailoress.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were Jewish. So her, her--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: --her executive staff were Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Executive staff was Jewish. We lived-- I was put into a-- I worked into a, in a factory who-- there were many, many, many ways of making this cotton. I was in a [NON-ENGLISH], but that was so full that you hardly could see.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was a cotton mill?

speaker: interviewee
text: A cotton mill, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your job? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had, I had to serve two big machines, much bigger than that room.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the machine--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working 12 hours. A shift was 12 hours, either from 6:00 in the morning till 6:00 at night or vice versa or from half past 1:00 at night till half past 1:00 at lunchtime and vice versa, too. And on top of it, we were treated like dogs in the camp with very, very little food, with no-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were in a room as big as this, maybe 50 of us on three bunks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had these tiered-- these bunks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And in the middle there was a, a big, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a round thing that everybody could, could do their business in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was like a, a hole in the middle of the room?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, like a bucket.

speaker: interviewer
text: A bucket.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember the bucket was always full in the night. And I could hear how, how it was dripping out. And maybe out of seven nights a week, five nights a week, I was taking this bucket out. I couldn't stand it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when we came home, we had to work hard scrubbing the floors, bringing the coal, peeling the potatoes, of which we-- the soup was made, just of the, of the peels of the potatoes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were hungry. I thought that in the whole world, there isn't enough bread that I can ever be satisfied. I was dreaming of potatoes. At this time at the beginning, I still was getting-- I, I forgot to tell you this. This is most important. I was getting letters from my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she knew that you were in Ober Altstadt.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camp. Yes. I wrote to my-- of course, I wrote. That was still a labor camp. There wasn't a concentration camp yet. That was a labor camp. And in that I wrote to my-- I wrote to her, but the first letter I got from my mother, and she say that she, she's ashamed to go on the street because people are laughing at what a stupid daughter she had. Her daughter was freed to go home from the concentration-- from, from the [NON-ENGLISH] and all the people which stayed in the orphanage, they'll be freed any day. Of course, I felt terrible, first of all that my mother was so upset. And after all, there wasn't a, a, a [INAUDIBLE] job where I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you thought maybe you'd made the wrong decision?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe I done wrong. But I, I thought, I'm young. And if I'm meant to survive, I'll survive here, too. And if not, so what's the difference where I survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few weeks later came a letter from my mother. And she writes, angel of life must have pushed you because all those people who stayed in the orphanage were stripped naked and barefoot and put in a bus. And for many years I didn't know what happened to that bus. You remember the film [INAUDIBLE]? Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to see the film [INAUDIBLE]. Do you remember-- I don't have to explain here what is the film [INAUDIBLE]. Because I suppose everybody knows. In that film, I saw the bus. Do you remember seeing the bus? And I was supposed to be in the bus. All the people who were in that bus, by the time they reached their destination, which was Chelmno, they were all dead because they, they, the pipe with the gas--

speaker: interviewer
text: The exhaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: The exhaust was, was taken, was taken into the, the compartment where the people were, and they all arrived dead. So I should have been dead 53 years ago. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] In the camp I was getting letters from my mother. I was getting parcels from my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: The, the Germans allowed the parcels to come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was in the parcels?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- bread, a piece of honey cake. She used then to send me [NON-ENGLISH], you know, like little parcels daily. And sometimes a piece of bread in it, and sometime with a piece of bread and some money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you translate [NON-ENGLISH] for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH]. A [NON-ENGLISH] is like [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] they wanted-- you know, if, if somebody has a business and wants to show his merchandise--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean a sample.

speaker: interviewee
text: A sample, only worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: With-- well, of no value.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of no value. A sample of no value.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so because she had written sample, no value on the parcel--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --it came to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: It came to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you got a little bit of money?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little bit of money sometimes and sometimes bread. You know, in the, in the bread was a little bit of money sometimes. But then, it was maybe 1943, and we knew that it-- we never-- I never knew about Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your mother write to you about what was happening to her? About her situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: She didn't write much, but I only know that once our Lagerfuhrer was going to, to our city, to Sosnowitz, to the [NON-ENGLISH], and I promised her that my mother will give her lots of jewelry if she bring her with, with her to our camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: You wanted the, the Lagerfuhrer, this Erma Hoffman--

speaker: interviewee
text: Erma Hoffman to bring my mother to the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother didn't want to. She said she's on a diet. She can't be in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had an opportunity to bring your mother to you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And she refused. Because the camp, that was the worst thing that, at this time, existed in my mother's--

speaker: interviewer
text: The thing, the thing that she knew about.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother was relatively OK all this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was OK, but she suffered a lot. She was-- she wasn't a well person. She always had severe arthritis, and she had kidney trouble and gall bladder trouble, all the Jewish troubles.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you maintained this correspondence--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: For how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till about August 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what, what happened with the correspondence then?

speaker: interviewee
text: In one of the last letters my mother wrote to me, I'm sending you a parcel. And keep the box of the parcel. She didn't say keep the box. She said, I'm making a special box to send you a parcel. And then the parcel arrived. I took whatever was in the parcel. And I threw the box out. And a couple of days later, I thought, maybe my mother made the parcel. It was a double decker. I never could find anymore this box. And I never know what my mother sent.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was stupid. But it doesn't matter. It-- this doesn't matter, only if there with things of some sentimental value, which would matter to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: After that, after your mother sent you the special box--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you hear from your mother again?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the letters and parcels stopped.

speaker: interviewee
text: They stopped, the letters. And then we became a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your arbeitslager was--

speaker: interviewee
text: It became-- it changed to a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed in the same place?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the same place all the time. Always at the--

speaker: interviewer
text: But, but the, the sort of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Concentration camp. We had to parade naked in front of the SS men. We, we had SS men, you know, [NON-ENGLISH] to watch us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Guards.

speaker: interviewee
text: To guard us. And the food became less and less, the treatment became worse and worse. The typhoid was just killing, left, right, and center, everybody, every one of us-- not everyone, because some survived. The tuberculosis, people were dying on tuberculosis. The bed bugs was eating us up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were just covered in these big bed bugs?

speaker: interviewee
text: The bed bugs was something, the smell of the bed bugs. We, we-- there was very little opportunity to clean it. But I still tried to clean the, the mattresses there that were full of straw, full of the bed bugs, but it didn't help much. But the worst thing was the lice. The lice. You know what this feels, lice?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: This, the lice, which occur here, the pubic lice, and they-- half of them are in your body and half is out. And they, they are so terrible. They, they, they-- you, you get meshuga from it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they drive you crazy.

speaker: interviewee
text: They drive you crazy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What? They're so itchy?

speaker: interviewee
text: So itchy. But I-- we had somebody there that shaved me, and that's how I got rid of those, of those pubic lice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once there was no hair--

speaker: interviewee
text: Once there was no hair--

speaker: interviewer
text: --that, that meant that, that you could get rid of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your own-- the hair on your head. Was that shaven? When the camp became a--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was short to here but not shaved. Only when somebody committed something, you know? A crime-- not a crime, but didn't do what, what was supposed to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, they misbehaved.

speaker: interviewee
text: Misbehave. As a punishment, they shaved.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you could see who had been in trouble--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --because their head had been shaven.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shaved. They shaved it in front of everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a, a punishment, a method of punishment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Method of punishment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your work change once the camp changed from being an arbeitslager to being a concentration camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the hours became longer.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did-- so, so you worked not 12-hour shifts now, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, most probably 12-hour shifts I worked in concentration camp. Maybe before I did-- I don't remember. But I know that the hours became longer, and we worked harder. And the treatment was so much worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say the treatment was worse, what sort of things-- how, how did-- what do you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you, you pass by, and they slapped you. And they, they treated you like a piece of shit. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No, you were not a person. You were a number. And you were not anymore. You know, before I was called Lola Majerczyk. Then I had a number. Not tattooed, but I had a number.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- you were referred to then--

speaker: interviewee
text: As a number.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your number?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think 21, 201, but I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had to answer to a number--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --not to a name. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Tell me-- and, and you worked in the, in the cotton spinning.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the cotton spinning.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did the same, same work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same work I did for three and a half years. And I was lucky so many times that I didn't-- I remember, I met a friend of mine which was with me. We, we were both-- we were like sisters in that camp. We worked together, every piece of bread we shared together, every piece of-- if we had any-- a shoe or anything, a, a frock we shared together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. She is in, in a, in Israel, and I'm-- in a kibbutz. And each time I'm in Israel, I visit her. And when I was last time there with my daughter, she said, did you tell your daughter what happened to, to us-- how you behaved? I say, I've never tell anything because I, I couldn't talk. For many years, I didn't say a thing. I didn't tell you even-- anyhow, she, she couldn't speak English. And my daughter couldn't understand English. My daughter couldn't understand Hebrew. And she didn't speak English.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she told my daughter, you know what your mother done? We used to have-- we used to work at the same machines, one next to another, and somebody was bringing us the merchandise that we were supposed to put on the, on the machine. And it was a Frenchman.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Frenchmen sometimes brought-- outside there, there were, there were prisoners of war of South African Englishmen. And we know we could eye them or something like this and sometimes they, they, they-- the Englishmen brought us-- the Frenchmen brought us a letter from one of those boyfriends of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: South Africans--

speaker: interviewee
text: South African. And we were-- we got this letter, and we started to read it. And the [NON-ENGLISH] called us.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. What was the [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: The [NON-ENGLISH] was the one who overlooked this particular hole where we were working.

speaker: interviewer
text: The foreman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. How did you say?

speaker: interviewer
text: Foreman.

speaker: interviewee
text: A foreman. More than a foreman, a meister.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was in charge of that whole--

speaker: interviewee
text: In charge, in charge of this. And he called it. And as he-- he, he couldn't read it because it was in English. And he said, I'm going to give it to the authorities and [SPEAKING GERMAN], which means it will cost the head, one of yours.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said to [NON-ENGLISH]-- [NON-ENGLISH] was-- had a sister in the camp. So I said to [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] if anybody goes, I go, because you have a sister. And that story she told my daughter. What, in fact, happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to the [NON-ENGLISH], and I said, look, I have in the next village here, which is also a concentration camp, I have a sister there. And she wrote me that letter in Polish. And he, he gave it back to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he didn't take the matter--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: --any further.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he didn't-- [SIGHS] And I tell you what, that I still consider myself pretty lucky. Being in the camps for three years and three months, I survived pretty good. I was lucky. I didn't get any of the-- at the end, the last days of the war, which I didn't know that they are the last days of the war, I felt that I am getting sick. I couldn't even eat what they gave me. And you know, you have such a specific tongue when we are getting paratyphoid. I had, I had the tongue already.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- what happens to your tongue?

speaker: interviewee
text: It, it has got a circle of red around it, a round circle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's one of the signs--

speaker: interviewee
text: A sign of the paratyphoid.

speaker: interviewer
text: But up until that very end time, you actually remained--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well.

speaker: interviewer
text: --relatively well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Relatively well. I was a strong one. When we were, when we were working outside and we were digging the trenches for the--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is different from the spinning? You changed work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, at the end, the last few months we worked outside at the-- digging trenches for the Germans. And I was so strong that I sometimes digged the biggest trenches, the biggest pieces out of, of stones out. And when the SS man saw it, he gave me a bit of soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you actually were a, a prize worker again. From being a prize knitter--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a prize worker there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you, you had the strength and, and the relative health--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: To continue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- were people dying all around you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. All around me, people were dying, but I hadn't seen a corpse.

speaker: interviewer
text: So would somebody die in the barracks at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the barrack. When they, when they-- when somebody was dying on tuberculosis, they took him out already half dead. They were dying in, in, in, in the sick rooms on, on typhoid, of malnutrition and everything. I saw them. I saw them. I saw people walking like corpses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell if people were going to die? Did you know that there were some people who were finished?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remember their name till today. I don't remember their alive names, but the one who died.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you somehow did not get typhus, and you did not get tuberculosis.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never had a headache.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you managed to keep--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I, I keep-- I keep pretty well.

speaker: interviewer
text: You, you mentioned the girl who you were so friendly with. Was she a friend from before, from home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: You met her in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I met her in the camp, and I became friendly with her in the camp. I knew her maybe by sight because she comes from the same city, but she didn't go to the same school. And I only knew kids which went to the same school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you, do you think that having somebody with you, like a very close friend, helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we, we, we used to give courage one another. And I knew that I can depend-- if I get-- if something-- if I, if I be hungry, that she will do anything to give me a scrap of bread. It, it was a lot. It meant a lot. But yet, on the other side, I met here a girl that was with me for three years and three months in the same room. And we were in Mount Buffalo, in the same, in the same, on the same holiday place for two weeks. We spoke to each other. We, we watched each other play games. And we didn't know who we are.

speaker: interviewer
text: The last day, somebody ask her, where did you-- where have you been during the war? And she said, I've been in the concentration camp. Which camp? Ober Altstadt. I said, I've been in Ober Altstadt. And that was [NON-ENGLISH]. She didn't recognize me. And I didn't recognize her. And yet, she had been in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Been in the same camp. And we were quite good friends in the same [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: The same room.

speaker: interviewee
text: The same room.

speaker: interviewer
text: All the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's stop.
